title: Paper Explained: When Prompts Override Vision: Prompt-Induced Hallucinations in LVLMs - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle: Grounding Vision: Reducing Prompt-Induced Hallucinations

content.background:
Large vision-language models can describe and reason about images with impressive accuracy, but they also sometimes say things that aren’t supported by what’s actually in the picture. This is a big problem in real-world use, where wrong statements can mislead people or cause mistakes in decision-making. Researchers previously argued it might come from either the “eyes” part (the vision backbone) or the “narrator” part (the language component), or from a mix of both, but they didn’t have a clear way to tell which factor mattered most. Because of that ambiguity, it was hard to know where to focus improvements to make these systems more trustworthy.

Why this mattered is that we can’t rely on flashy numbers or impressive tasks alone. If a model can hit high scores on some tests but still fill in gaps with generic background knowledge or prompt-driven guesses, it won’t be dependable when faced with new images or different prompts. In particular, prompts and instructions given to the model can biasedly push it to rely on textual priors rather than the actual visual evidence, leading to prompt-induced hallucinations. Without a simple, controlled way to separate the influence of the image from the influence of language and prompts, researchers and developers risk chasing the wrong fixes or underestimating the safety risks.

The authors argue that we need a dedicated benchmark to untangle these sources of error. By introducing HalluScope, they want to measure how much hallucination comes from text priors, from the vision component, or from instruction-driven biases, so the community can target the right part of the system. This motivation goes beyond academic curiosity: it’s about building LVLMs that are more grounded, reliable, and safer to use in real-world settings. To help the field move forward, they also plan to share the benchmark, data, and code so others can reproduce findings and push the research toward more trustworthy visual-language systems.
content.methodology:
LVLMs (large vision-language models) can describe what they see, but they sometimes make up things that aren’t in the image. This paper introduces HalluScope, a new way to test and separate the different reasons a model might “hallucinate.” The big finding is that a lot of these hallucinations come from the model leaning on textual priors and instructions (what it was told or trained to think), rather than from the actual visual input. In other words, prompts and background knowledge can push the model to give grounded-looking but false answers.

How HalluScope works conceptually (what they did and why it matters)
- They treat the problem like a detective game: ask the model to answer with different styles or prompts and see when it relies on the image versus on what the text already “knows.” This helps separate contributions from the vision part, the language part, and the prompt/priors.
- They categorize sources of hallucination into things like textual instruction priors (the specific prompts you give or the training signals) and general background knowledge, and compare them against the model’s actual visual input.
- The result is a benchmark that can reveal which factors are most responsible for hallucinations, showing that instruction-driven priors play a large role.

What HalluVL-DPO is and how it works at a high level
- HalluVL-DPO is a fine-tuning framework designed to make LVLMs more visually grounded when answering. Think of it as teaching the model to “trust the picture first, then speak.”
- It uses preference optimization: create a curated training dataset where grounded (image-based) answers are preferred over non-grounded ones. The model learns to favor answers that stay anchored to what the image shows.
- The workflow is: collect the dataset, train a preference model that rates grounded versus ungrounded responses, and then adjust the LVLM to prefer grounded outputs during generation. The aim is to reduce the influence of textual instructions on what the model says, without hurting its other abilities.

What this buys and how they validate it
- The approach specifically targets the instruction-driven hallucination channel and shows that the model becomes more visually grounded after fine-tuning, while still performing well on other tasks and staying robust on other hallucination tests.
- They plan to release the benchmark, the curated dataset for preferences, and the code so others can reproduce and extend the work.
- In plain terms: HalluScope helps us understand where the problem comes from, and HalluVL-DPO gives us a practical way to retrain models to “read the image first” more reliably, making them less prone to misleading prompts.
content.results:
This work tackles a big problem with vision-language models: they sometimes say things that aren’t grounded in what they actually see. To study why, the authors built HalluScope, a benchmark designed to tease apart different sources of hallucination (like the image part, the language part, and the prompts given to the model). Using HalluScope, they found that most of these hallucinations come from textual priors—basically the model leaning on general knowledge or instructions it was told to follow—rather than from the vision system itself. In short, prompts and background text often drive the model to “make stuff up,” even when the image doesn’t support it. This shifts the focus from only improving vision sensors to also managing how prompts and text guide the model.

To address this, they propose HalluVL-DPO, a practical fine-tuning framework that makes existing LVLMs more visually grounded. The idea is simple: use preference optimization to train the model to prefer grounded, image-supported answers over hallucinated ones. They build a carefully chosen training dataset to teach this preference, so the model learns to give responses that reflect what is actually seen in the image. The result is a model that reduces the targeted hallucination without hurting— and sometimes even improving—performance on other tests of vision and language abilities.

The work matters practically because it offers a way to improve reliability of LVLMs without starting from scratch. You can fine-tune off-the-shelf models to be more careful about grounding, which is important for real‑world use where incorrect claims can be costly. Plus, the authors plan to publicly release the HalluScope benchmark, the preference dataset, and the code, helping researchers and developers reproduce the results and build safer, more trustworthy systems. Overall, this work identifies a key source of errors and provides a concrete, scalable method to mitigate it, advancing the practical use of vision-language AI.
content.significance:
- This paper matters today because it tackles a real danger in multimodal AI: hallucinations that come from prompts, not just from the vision part of the model. The authors show that many “language priors” introduced by textual instructions can lead LVLMs to describe or reason about an image in ways that aren’t grounded in what the image actually shows. By introducing HalluScope, they give researchers a way to systematically diagnose where hallucinations come from. They also demonstrate a practical fix, HalluVL-DPO, that tunes models to prefer grounded, visually supported answers over those prompted by prior knowledge. For real-world tools like AI assistants with vision (examples you’ve likely seen in ChatGPT with image input, GPT-4V-like systems, or image-enabled copilots), this matters because users rely on accurate, image-based understanding rather than stylish but incorrect descriptions.

- In the long run, the paper helps shift AI research from chasing tiny gains in perceptual encoders to improving alignment between vision and language. It emphasizes grounding a model’s outputs in actual visual evidence, even when strong textual instructions are present. The preference-based fine-tuning approach (a form of DPO/RLHF-like training) has influenced how researchers and companies think about making multimodal systems safer and more trustworthy: use curated, grounding-focused data to teach models to prefer factual visual grounding over priors. HalluScope-inspired benchmarks also push the field toward standardized ways to measure and compare ways to reduce prompt-induced hallucinations, which accelerates progress across labs and industry.

- For applications and systems people know today, this work feeds into safer, more reliable multimodal AI in education, design, accessibility tools, healthcare-supportive robotics, and consumer assistants. It helps explain why some image-captioning or VQA systems can sound confident but be wrong, and it offers concrete methods to curb that behavior without sacrificing overall capability. By releasing datasets and code, the authors also help the broader community reproduce and extend improvements. In the near future, you’ll see more LVLMs and multimodal assistants that explicitly address prompt-induced priors, delivering grounded, trustworthy image understanding in tools you use every day.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Preference Optimization: The Heart of When Prompts Override Vision
concept_explanation.content:
Imagine you’re a student who is looking at a photo and answering questions about what’s in it. Sometimes you rely on what you already know from textbooks or common sense (textual priors), not just what the photo shows. If the question asks about a scene that isn’t clearly visible, you might confidently give an answer that sounds plausible but isn’t actually grounded in the image. That’s the kind of problem large vision-language models (LVLMs) face: they can produce convincing text that isn’t truly supported by the visual input. The paper tackles this with a technique called preference optimization, specifically inside a framework they name HalluVL-DPO.

Here’s how preference optimization works in this context, step by step. First, you create a training setup where the model is shown prompts with images, and for each prompt you collect outputs that differ in how grounded they are to the image. Some outputs are carefully grounded—they strictly reflect what the image shows—while others reveal more of the model’s background knowledge or the wording of the prompt, which can lead to hallucinations. Next, you train a separate “preference” signal (a small scoring model or a simple ranking system) that learns to say “Output A is better than Output B” when A is more grounded in the image than B. Finally, you fine-tune the base LVLM using this preference signal so that, when given a new image and prompt, it tends to produce the grounded, image-consistent answer rather than a text-prior-driven hallucination. In short: teach the model to prefer grounded answers over ones that sound plausible but aren’t anchored in what the image shows.

A concrete example helps. Suppose you show the LVLM a photo of a cat sitting on a windowsill and ask, “What is on the windowsill?” A grounded answer would be “a cat on a windowsill.” A less grounded, more priors-based answer might say “a cat and a fish bowl,” if the prompt nudges the model to bring in typical home decor. During preference optimization, you’d have pairs like (grounded answer, hallucinated answer) and teach the model to prefer the grounded one. Over many such examples, the LVLM learns to rely more on the actual pixels in the image and less on general textual priors baked into its training data or the prompt’s wording. The result is a model that answers more accurately about what it sees, even when the prompt tries to steer it toward common-but-not-true-in-this-image conclusions.

Why is this approach important? Because it directly tackles the main failure mode of LVLMs: prompt-induced hallucinations. By explicitly teaching the model to value groundedness, you can reduce errors that arise when the model’s memory or instructions overpower the visual evidence. This has practical impact across fields where accuracy matters and visual cues must be trusted, such as assisting visually impaired users with image descriptions, analyzing medical or scientific images, or supporting autonomous systems that must reason about what they actually observe rather than what they think is usually true. The authors also couple this with HalluScope, a benchmark that helps quantify where hallucinations come from, so researchers can see how well preference optimization is working and ensure it doesn’t hurt other abilities.

In short, preference optimization in HalluVL-DPO is a targeted fine-tuning approach: build a signal that judges groundedness, create a training set that contrasts grounded versus non-grounded outputs, and tune the model so it prefers grounded answers. This keeps LVLMs honest about what the image shows while preserving or even boosting their performance on other tasks. By making the model trust the visual input more than the plain text priors, this method offers a practical path toward safer, more reliable multimodal AI in real-world applications.

summary: This paper introduces HalluScope, a benchmark that reveals prompt-induced hallucinations in LVLMs are mainly caused by textual priors, and HalluVL-DPO, a preference-based fine-tuning method that makes models rely more on vision, thereby reducing hallucinations while preserving or improving visual capabilities.
